chain_of_thought: Top frame is __memcpy, below it are Decode_DER_ASN1_ST → CERT_Load → Java_com_ubikey_jni_UbikeyJni_jGetSubjectName → fuzz_one_input → main. The allocator/sanitizer frame is the C library memcpy; the first app-level frame below it is Decode_DER_ASN1_ST in libUbikeyJni.so.

A memcpy crash during DER/ASN.1 decoding almost always indicates a bounds issue: copying a length-specified field into a fixed/assumed buffer without sufficient validation. The function name Decode_DER_ASN1_ST suggests a custom ASN.1 structure decoder, not a generic hardened library. CERT_Load is the higher-level certificate loader that likely passes raw DER data and lengths to the decoder.

Even though I cannot decompile the actual functions due to tool errors, the stack shape and naming indicate app code directly driving memcpy with data parsed from DER. In a fuzz harness, fuzz_one_input typically feeds attacker-controlled bytes into the JNI method; here, Java_com_ubikey_jni_UbikeyJni_jGetSubjectName presumably takes a certificate or byte array from Java and passes it down to CERT_Load, which in turn calls Decode_DER_ASN1_ST. There is no evidence of robust length validation in between; the crash in memcpy is consistent with a length derived from untrusted ASN.1 length fields exceeding the actual allocation.

Because this occurs within a production library (libUbikeyJni.so) of a banking app (kr.co.busanbank.mbp), and the same code is used in normal operation to parse certificates (e.g., from smart cards, tokens, or potentially network-provided cert chains), untrusted input controlling certificate contents is realistic. Malformed certificates are a standard attacker vector. Thus the bug is likely reachable in real use, not only via the synthetic fuzz harness.

The behavior is characteristic of a classic out-of-bounds read/write via unchecked memcpy (CWE-787/CWE-119). While exact direction (read vs write) is unknown, crashing memcpy implies accessing invalid memory, which is exploitable in principle. Therefore I classify this as a genuine memory-safety vulnerability with high severity, but with slightly less than full certainty due to lack of direct code view.
reasons: Crash in __memcpy called from Decode_DER_ASN1_ST during certificate loading indicates unchecked memory copy based on parsed data lengths
Input path from fuzz_one_input → JNI → CERT_Load → Decode_DER_ASN1_ST implies attacker-controlled DER/ASN.1 data influences memcpy length or pointers
Occurs in app-owned libUbikeyJni.so, not a system library, so bug is in application code and would execute in production when parsing certificates
recommendations: Manually review Decode_DER_ASN1_ST and CERT_Load around all memcpy/memmove calls to ensure destination buffers are sized and bounds-checked against parsed length fields
Harden ASN.1/DER parsing by validating all length fields, using constant-time, bounds-checked decoding helpers or a well-tested crypto/ASN.1 library instead of custom code
Re-run fuzzing with ASAN/UBSAN and corpus minimization to confirm the exact crashing input and pinpoint the vulnerable copy site
assumptions: DER/ASN.1 data reaching CERT_Load is at least partially attacker-controlled under realistic app workflows (e.g., user/provided certificates or tokens)
Decode_DER_ASN1_ST implements custom parsing logic rather than delegating to a fully validated third-party library
The memcpy crash is due to out-of-bounds access rather than a prior heap corruption by unrelated code
limitations: Could not decompile libUbikeyJni.so functions due to MCP tool errors, so specific code snippets and exact variables are unknown
No Java-side call graph details were available to verify exact argument types and validations in jGetSubjectName
Lack of register/memory dump prevents determining whether overflow is on source or destination buffer side

chain_of_thought: Top native frame is Compute_ASN1_ST_ChildNum inside libUbikeyJni.so, below that are certificate conversion and load functions, then the JNI bridge Java_com_ubikey_jni_UbikeyJni_jGetSubjectName and fuzz_one_input. The process termination reason mentions Compute_ASN1_ST_ChildNum, suggesting it crashed or aborted inside this ASN.1 child-processing helper.

Given the absence of concrete decompiled code, I infer Compute_ASN1_ST_ChildNum walks ASN.1 structures to compute the number or index of a child node while parsing an X.509 certificate. Such helpers are classic sources of memory safety issues if length/offset handling is wrong. The surrounding functions Convert_ASN1_to_X509_TBS_CERT, Convert_ASN1_to_X509_CERT, and CERT_Load form a typical certificate parsing pipeline, driven by certificate bytes ultimately controlled by caller input.

The fuzz harness entry fuzz_one_input suggests the fuzzer supplies arbitrary data (e.g., a byte array representing a certificate) into CERT_Load via the JNI bridge. In a real app, those bytes would realistically come from untrusted sources (network, smart card, NFC, etc.), so ASN.1 parsing must be robust to malformed input. Thus, untrusted data clearly flows into the ASN.1 parser stack that reaches Compute_ASN1_ST_ChildNum.

The process termination message doesn’t say this is a harness-induced assert (like explicitly aborting on null JNI env) nor a sanitizer-only abort. Instead it points at an internal helper, consistent with a crash from out-of-bounds read/write or invalid pointer/length math on ASN.1 structures. These are well-known vulnerability classes in certificate parsers, often leading to exploitable memory corruption (CWE-787/125).

There is no indication that JNI or Java side validate or constrain the ASN.1 structure itself beyond maybe passing a buffer and its length; even if length is bounded, malformed internal ASN.1 lengths/child indices can still trigger logic errors in Compute_ASN1_ST_ChildNum. Fuzzing specifically found a terminating condition in this helper, which is unlikely to be purely environmental because all frames are from libUbikeyJni.so (no obvious harness misuse like passing null env or invalid JNI object).

Given typical ASN.1 parsing risks, the fact that the crash is within core parsing routines, the realistic attacker control over certificate bytes, and the lack of evidence this is just a benign assert or unreachable harness-only path, I assess this as a likely genuine vulnerability in ASN.1 child computation logic. Exploitability is plausibly high (memory safety in parser), but without disassembly I can’t prove exact primitive; still the context strongly suggests an out-of-bounds access or similar. Confidence is not maximal due to missing code, but above 0.6 due to strong circumstantial evidence.
reasons: Crash occurs in ASN.1 parsing helper Compute_ASN1_ST_ChildNum within certificate conversion pipeline, a classic source of memory safety bugs.
Input path (fuzz_one_input → JNI jGetSubjectName → CERT_Load → ASN.1 parser) implies attacker-controlled certificate bytes can reach the crashing function.
No signs crash is due solely to JNI harness misuse or impossible real-world conditions; all frames are internal to libUbikeyJni.so.
Certificate/ASN.1 parsers are historically vulnerable to out-of-bounds and length/child-index handling issues (CWE-787/CWE-125), consistent with crash location and name.
recommendations: Review and audit Compute_ASN1_ST_ChildNum, Convert_ASN1_to_X509_TBS_CERT, and related ASN.1 parsing code for bounds/length checks on child indices and constructed lengths.
Add robust validation of ASN.1 structure before or during traversal; ensure all index and offset computations are checked against buffer bounds and child counts.
Run targeted fuzzing on CERT_Load and ASN.1 parser with sanitizers (ASan/UBSan) to reproduce and precisely localize the memory safety issue, then add regression tests for the crashing inputs.
assumptions: fuzz_one_input passes arbitrary or minimally checked certificate bytes into CERT_Load via the JNI bridge.
Compute_ASN1_ST_ChildNum performs traversal/indexing in ASN.1 structures and is capable of out-of-bounds memory access if child counts or lengths are inconsistent.
The same code path is reachable in production when the app processes external certificates or UBikey data.
limitations: No decompiled native code available to confirm the exact faulty instruction or memory access primitive.
Java-side argument construction and validation for jGetSubjectName is not visible; details of length constraints are inferred from naming and stack trace.
Exact crash reason (e.g., SIGSEGV vs. explicit abort) is not provided, limiting certainty about exploitability.

chain_of_thought: Stack shows __memcpy at top, then Decode_DER_ASN1_ST → Get_DER_Child → Convert_ASN1_to_X509_CERT → CERT_Load → JNI bridge. So first app-level native frame below libc is Decode_DER_ASN1_ST (and maybe CERT_Load as higher level). We lack direct disassembly due to tool failure, so must reason from naming and context only.

Crash in __memcpy almost always indicates out-of-bounds read/write or overlap misuse. In ASN.1 / X.509 parsing code, memcpy is typically used to move DER fields into internal buffers or structures. If length/offset come from parsed DER without-full validation, this is classic memory-corruption bug terrain.

The library map shows all relevant functions coming from libUbikeyJni.so, so this is app’s own native code, not just a harness artifact. JNI method jGetSubjectName presumably takes some certificate input (in production, likely from smart card / USB token or server certificate). In fuzz harness, fuzz_one_input likely feeds arbitrary certificate bytes into CERT_Load or equivalent path, simulating malformed/untrusted certificates.

Key question: is memcpy length or pointer derived from attacker-controlled data without sufficient bound checks? Since we cannot decompile, we inspect names: Decode_DER_ASN1_ST suggests generic DER decoder, Get_DER_Child suggests navigation among ASN.1 elements by offset/length, Convert_ASN1_to_X509_CERT suggests building a C struct representation, CERT_Load likely wrapper taking raw cert bytes and size. All of these are typical locations for parsing issues if code is custom and not using robust libraries like OpenSSL.

Given function naming, CERT_Load is likely passed (uint8_t *buf, size_t len) from Java or from higher layers. In production, certificate data can indeed be attacker-controlled (e.g., remote server certificate, certificate file loaded from storage, or data from NFC, etc.). Fuzzer harness likely mimics that by passing arbitrary blobs. If memcpy inside Decode_DER_ASN1_ST crashed, it suggests that the function believed it had a valid pointer+length but underlying heap bounds were violated. That generally means there was insufficient checking of element length vs overall buffer or target buffer.

Alternative is that harness passed a NULL / extremely small buffer and bogus length, but then CERT_Load should either trust the given length (and crash): that is still a bug, but we need to see if such a call is possible in real code. The JNI jGetSubjectName path probably gets a byte[] or String from Java (certificate or filename). In the absence of validation (and typical cert-loading APIs allow arbitrary certificates), an attacker who controls that Java-level input via network or file can propagate completely arbitrary bytes to native parser. Bounds-checking should still prevent crashes; failing that is a vulnerability.

Nothing in the stack suggests explicit harness-only behavior like fuzz-only entrypoints in the native lib. Fuzz harness entry is external (fuzz_one_input), but it simply triggers the same CERT_Load → Convert_ASN1_to_X509_CERT path as app does when calling jGetSubjectName. The fact that the crashing function is named Decode_DER_ASN1_ST and there is a direct memcpy call implies a parser bug, not an artificial harness misuse (like passing NULL JNIEnv).

Without binary we cannot show exact memcpy arguments, but function naming, call stack structure, and the nature of the crash (allocator-independent memcpy fault) align strongly with classic out-of-bounds read/write due to incorrect DER length/offset handling. Since certificate/ASN.1 data is untrusted and realistically attacker-controlled, this satisfies definition of vulnerability. Potential impact is memory corruption (OOB write/read) in a security-sensitive JNI library used for certificate handling / authentication, so severity is at least high, possibly critical. But without proof of write vs read and exploit controllability, classify as high with 0.8 confidence rather than near-1.0.
reasons: Crash occurs in __memcpy called from Decode_DER_ASN1_ST while parsing DER/ASN.1 certificate data, strongly indicating an out-of-bounds memory operation.
Call stack shows CERT_Load → Convert_ASN1_to_X509_CERT → Decode_DER_ASN1_ST processing attacker-controlled certificate bytes provided via JNI method jGetSubjectName.

No evidence of fuzz-only misuse; fuzz harness calls into the same certificate-loading path the app would use, so malformed external certificates could trigger the same memcpy-based crash in production.
recommendations: Manually reverse-engineer Decode_DER_ASN1_ST, Get_DER_Child, and Convert_ASN1_to_X509_CERT to locate the memcpy call and ensure all length/offset values are validated against the source buffer and destination buffer sizes.
Add strict DER/ASN.1 bounds checks (element length vs remaining buffer, child offsets within parent, maximum certificate size) before performing memcpy/memmove and fail gracefully on malformed input.

Consider reusing a well-tested ASN.1/X.509 parsing library (e.g., OpenSSL, BoringSSL) instead of custom parsers, or at least add fuzz tests with ASAN/UBSAN on the native library to catch similar issues.
assumptions: Certificate or ASN.1 data consumed by CERT_Load is attacker-controlled in realistic app scenarios (e.g., from network, file, smart card).
The crashing memcpy in __memcpy is due to invalid size or pointer derived from untrusted DER fields rather than a deliberate harness-only misuse such as forced NULL pointers.
JNI method jGetSubjectName in production passes certificate bytes/length to CERT_Load without drastic sanitisation that would preclude malformed ASN.1 structures.
limitations: Could not decompile libUbikeyJni.so; memcpy arguments and exact bounds checks are inferred from naming and stack trace, not directly observed.
We do not see the Java-side implementation of UbikeyJni.jGetSubjectName; there might be additional validation or constraints on certificate input not visible here.
Impact (read vs write OOB, exploitability for RCE) cannot be precisely determined without binary and runtime state; classification based on typical patterns in custom ASN.1 parsers.
